drills.scenario: A local gym wants to run a cold-traffic Facebook ad for a weight-loss bootcamp. The owner drafts the primary text: "Do you want to lose 30 lbs in 30 days? Sign up today!"
drills.question: Why will this copy get the ad disapproved, and what is the compliant fix?
options: It's fine as written — direct results claims are allowed for fitness offers

It violates Policy on two counts (a guaranteed audience result "lose 30 lbs in 30 days" and tying "you" to a personal attribute); reframe as the owner's own story, e.g., "How I lost 28 lbs in six weeks"
The only problem is it's too short; add 1,000 words of proof to make it compliant
Replace "you" with all-caps "YOU WILL LOSE 30 LBS" to grab attention
drills.explanation: Facebook bans direct claims/promises about the audience ("Lose 30 lbs in 30 Days" gets banned) and bans tying "you" to a personal attribute ("Do you want to lose weight?"). The compliant move is to share your OWN results ("how I lost 28 lbs in six weeks"). Length and all-caps don't fix a Policy violation.

drills.scenario: A productivity coach is writing a long-copy educational ad for a Problem/Solution-aware cold audience (UPSYD, Steps 2-3) to move people from awareness toward intent.
drills.question: Put the sections of the long-copy educational ad in the correct order.
options: Open with a contrarian "Big Aha" hook question
Provide value — debunk the myths the prospect believes
Introduce the solution
Provide further proof
Pivot into the pitch
drills.explanation: UPSYD long-copy educational structure (Steps 2-3): (1) contrarian Big Aha hook question, (2) provide value/debunk myths, (3) introduce the solution, (4) provide further proof, (5) pivot into the pitch. Lead with the hook to earn the read; pitch only after you've delivered value and proof.

drills.scenario: A Shopify pet brand sells a checkout/upsell app to store owners. The copywriter keeps describing the app's features: "one-click upsells, drag-and-drop builder, A/B testing dashboard."
drills.question: Per the 7 Ad Building Blocks (Block #3, Benefits and Desires), what's the strongest reframe?
options: List even more features so the ad looks comprehensive
Append "so you can" to surface the deep desire — e.g., sell the outcome ("double your sales") not the software, speaking to the conversation in the prospect's mind
Remove all benefits and only state the price
Cite the literacy statistic that 50% of US adults can't read at 8th-grade level
drills.explanation: Block #3 (Benefits and Desires): surface the deep desire by appending "so you can"/"so that" to any benefit, and speak to the conversation in the prospect's mind, not the product features — exactly like Samcart selling checkout software but advertising "doubling your sales." Features don't sell; the desired outcome does.

drills.scenario: A SaaS founder runs a 4-minute video ad selling a webinar. The script saves the only call-to-action for the final 30 seconds, and the primary text is dense with adjectives like "best," "finest," and "outstanding." Watch time and conversions are both weak.
drills.question: Diagnose the two biggest fixes the module prescribes.
options: Get at least a soft CTA into the first 60-90 seconds, and cut superlatives/hyperbole (favor action verbs over adjectives, roughly 2:1)
Make every word ALL-CAPS and add more adjectives for emphasis
Lengthen the video to 10 minutes and remove the CTA entirely
Copy a competitor's winning script word-for-word to save time
drills.explanation: For video ads ~3 min+, get at least a soft CTA in the first 60-90 seconds (Block #6) — don't bury it. And avoid superlatives/hyperbole (best, finest, outstanding); keep adjectives minimal with action verbs over adjectives at roughly 2:1 (Element 7). All-caps everything is scammy (Element 10) and copying verbatim is ineffective (Element 1).

drills.scenario: A meal-kit brand's ad got tons of social shares but almost no sales. The team is debating what to change, since people clearly like the post.
drills.question: Using the troubleshooting guidance, what's the most likely root cause and fix?
options: The audience is too small — just raise the budget and shares will convert
High shares prove it's converting; leave the copy alone and wait
The next step was unclear — revamp the copy with a strong offer/CTA and add a URL to the copy; engagement without a clear next step doesn't convert
Add before/after weight-loss photos to boost trust
drills.explanation: The module's troubleshooting case is exactly this: an ad with high social shares wasn't converting until the copy was revamped with a strong offer/CTA and a URL added to the copy — then it converted "like crazy." Always make the next step in the journey crystal clear; engagement is not conversion.

drills.scenario: A meditation app is writing a short-copy ad and wants to use the 13 Elements of Persuasive Ad Copy as "seasonings" without triggering Policy or looking scammy.
drills.question: Which techniques are correct applications of the Elements? (Select all that apply.)
options: Capitalize only the ONE phrase you want remembered, e.g., "FREE TRAINING"
Write at roughly a 5th-grade reading level to "stop the thumb scroll"
Use an ellipsis to create a broken thought resolved on the next line ("...while they last")
All-caps the entire primary text so nothing is missed
Tie "you" to a personal attribute like "Do you have anxiety?" to qualify people
drills.explanation: Capitalize only the one key word/phrase (Element 10), write for Homer at ~5th-grade level (Element 3), and embrace the ellipsis to open a loop resolved on the next line (Element 11). All-caps everything is scammy and Policy-flagged; tying "you" to a personal attribute ("Do you have anxiety?") is a Policy violation (Element 8).

drills.scenario: A B2B consultant's ad has a low Relevance Score. They want to fix it by improving message-to-market match before touching budget or audience.
drills.question: Order the steps for fixing a low Relevance Score via message-to-market match.
options: Confirm the offer is actually what people want
Rewrite the copy to hit their biggest pains, desires, and fears
Move the prospect from the undesirable BEFORE state to the desirable AFTER state
drills.explanation: Fix a low Relevance Score by fixing message-to-market match: (1) confirm the offer is what people actually want, (2) rewrite copy to hit their biggest pains/desires/fears, (3) move the prospect from the undesirable BEFORE state to the desirable AFTER state. Start with the offer itself before reworking the language.

drills.scenario: A storytelling-style ad for a financial app weaves a narrative about "Jeff" applying for a line of credit. The writer is tempted to resolve the story's ending early and place the CTA afterward, separate from the story.
drills.question: Per the 7 Power Questions (open loops / Zeigarnik effect), what should they do instead?
options: Close the story as early as possible so readers feel satisfied before the CTA
Keep the story open and let its closure land AT the point of sale; closing too early switches off attention
Remove the character "Jeff" and address the reader as "you" throughout to call them out
Replace the story with a list of product features
drills.explanation: Power Question 6 / the Zeigarnik effect: open loops hold attention, so weave the sales message mid-story and let the story's closure land AT the point of sale. Closing the story too early triggers cognitive closure and switches the brain off. Telling a story through a fictitious character like "Jeff" also beats calling readers out with "you."

drills.scenario: An online course seller runs a strong, professionally produced selling video. They're now deciding how much primary text (copy length) to pair with it.
drills.question: Which copy-length choice best fits this situation?
options: Long-ass copy (1,000+ words) so the text re-sells everything the video already said
Short copy (~3-5 lines) — a solid selling video needs LESS copy because the video does the selling
Ultra-short of exactly one line, with no offer or CTA
Maximum length always wins regardless of creative
drills.explanation: Guidance from Copy Length / Mix It Up: a solid selling video needs LESS copy (short, ~3-5 lines) because the video does the selling. Long/long-ass copy is for image ads selling a core product or consult call and big cold-traffic asks — not for ads carried by a strong video.
